: March 7th 2026:
Sunday - March 1, Repository Housekeeping, Full Enterprise Brand Redesign Across 64 Frontend Files, Component-Layer Dark Mode Remediation, Deep Multi-Dimensional Audit, and Complete Send Page Feature Module Implementation

Objective: Execute a comprehensive repository cleanup removing approximately 49 MB of stale development artifacts, unify the visual identity of a wallet application by redesigning all 44 pages and 20 components with a semantic design token system ensuring dark mode compatibility, remediate approximately 727 component-layer colour violations, conduct a seven-dimensional deep audit fixing all actionable findings, and implement the complete send page feature module including a fiat currency conversion engine, a seven-state security verification step, recipient quick-select chips, and a five-step transfer flow.

Repository Cleanup and Progress Tracker Alignment
The day began with removal of 49 MB of accumulated development artifacts including 26 PNG screenshots, a 46 MB browser data directory, an 11 MB audit screenshot directory, empty test directories, and stale test result directories. The .gitignore was updated to include .next/ and a root-level *.png catch-all. The project progress tracker was updated from version 1.0.0 to 2.0.0 reflecting accurate completion status of five modules alongside four pending modules with phase assignments, complexity ratings, and dependency relationships. A live verification session against the development database confirmed face score normalisation, SLA badge colour coding, and storage URL prefix stripping were functioning correctly with real data.

Enterprise Brand Design System Deployment Across All Frontend Pages
A critical visual inconsistency was identified where the dashboard and send pages featured polished enterprise styling while every other page retained legacy patterns with hardcoded colours that fail in dark mode. A multi-agent orchestration with six parallel agents handled 14 logical phases of redesign, replacing approximately 400 hardcoded grey colour references with semantic tokens, converting all status indicator colours to opacity-based patterns, removing approximately 20 min-h-screen wrappers and 12 sticky header patterns, replacing approximately 15 direct icon imports with the centralised icon library, and standardising heading typography. The work spanned settings pages, a full-height chat interface across seven files, an SVG relationship graph visualisation, a context selector, five entity hub pages, nine government treasury sub-pages with three shared layouts, and twelve entity detail pages. The icon library was extended with 19 new exports. The effort produced 25 atomic commits modifying 64 files with approximately 5,200 lines changed.

Component-Layer Dark Mode Remediation
A grep audit revealed approximately 727 remaining hardcoded colour violations in the shared component layer. Two waves of parallel agents were deployed: the first wave of six agents addressed UI primitives across 13 files, a 2,346-line mega-component with 178 violations, context-switching and file-upload components, user detail modals, security-related admin API auth guards across 13 files, and graph fixes across 18 files. The second wave of five agents targeted approximately 400 remaining violations across analytics dashboards, registration wizards, transfer modals, navigation components, and authentication pages. The final result reduced 727 violations to 9 intentional exceptions with zero TypeScript errors across 22 atomic commits.

Seven-Dimension Deep Audit and Comprehensive Remediation
Seven parallel audit agents examined gap analysis, security, responsive design, Playwright UI testing, 2026 best practices, accessibility and performance, and code quality. The audit identified 4 high-severity security findings including a testing bypass lacking a production environment guard and a commented-out admin authentication guard, 2 critical and 8 high-severity responsive design issues including invalid Tailwind classes, 4 critical best-practice violations including useSearchParams without Suspense boundaries, and 3 critical accessibility issues including absent prefers-reduced-motion support. Five parallel fix agents addressed all actionable findings: security fixes added production environment guards and restored the admin authentication guard, accessibility fixes removed viewport scaling restrictions for WCAG 1.4.4 compliance and added a prefers-reduced-motion media query, responsive fixes corrected all invalid Tailwind classes, and performance fixes eliminated duplicate notification polling and lazy-loaded development tools. Six language specification corrections updated product terminology. The audit and fix round produced 34 additional atomic commits across 120 files.

Send Page Feature Module -- Fiat Conversion Engine
A self-contained currency module was created with five files housing 174 genesis currencies with symbols, codes, names, and gold price definitions. The conversion mathematics leverages the protocol's principle of one unit equalling one gram of gold, requiring only deterministic multiplication by each currency's pricePerGramOfGold value with no API calls or network dependency. The formatting layer handles zero decimal places for Japanese Yen, three for Bahraini Dinar, and two for standard currencies. A responsive currency selector renders as a popover on desktop and a bottom sheet on mobile using a 20-line SSR-safe useMediaQuery hook with three tiers: auto-detected currency, popular currencies, and a full searchable list.

Send Page Feature Module -- Security Verification Step and Five-Step Flow
A security verification step was implemented as a seven-state finite state machine at approximately 460 lines handling PIN not configured, PIN locked with countdown, PIN entry with six-digit masked input, PIN verified with 2FA required, PIN verified without 2FA when below threshold, 2FA entry with TOTP and backup code toggle, and authorised. One new API route was created for backup code verification. The transfer flow was extended from four steps to five by inserting the security step between intent and blockchain action. Fiat equivalents were threaded through the review and receipt steps.

Send Page Feature Module -- Recipient Chips and Amount Input Rewrite
A recipient quick-select component fetches beneficiaries via cached query, deduplicates by address, sorts by last-used timestamp, and renders the top five as clickable pills. The amount input was rewritten from 195 lines to approximately 465 lines implementing a bidirectional dual-input system for simultaneous GX and fiat entry, solving infinite update loops using lastEditedField and isInternalChange refs. Quick amount buttons gained fiat labels, fee preview gained fiat equivalents, and remaining balance gained fiat display. The implementation created 13 new files with approximately 1,510 lines of new code and modified 8 existing files with approximately 370 lines changed, addressing all 44 acceptance criteria with zero TypeScript errors.

Outcome: The repository was cleaned of 49 MB of artifacts, the entire wallet application achieved unified visual identity across all 44-plus pages with full dark mode support through 727 violation remediations, a seven-dimension audit produced 34 commits addressing security, accessibility, responsive design, performance, and best practices, and the complete send page feature module was implemented with a 174-currency fiat conversion engine, a seven-state security verification flow, recipient quick-select chips, and a five-step transfer integration across 31 new and modified files with zero TypeScript errors.

: March 7th 2026:
Tuesday - March 3, Send Page Acceptance Criteria Resolution, Iterative UX Overhaul with Branded Input System, Global Design Token Architecture, Mobile Typography Tuning, 100-Plus Finding Remediation, Backend Fee Calculation Endpoint, and End-to-End Transfer Flow Verification

Objective: Resolve four acceptance criteria gaps in the send page security verification step including a critical two-factor authentication logic bug, execute an iterative UX overhaul transforming input fields to enterprise brand design language, architect a centralised design token layer eliminating per-component style duplication, conduct a Playwright-driven typography audit across four viewports, systematically remediate 100-plus findings from a ten-agent deep audit, implement a Clean Architecture fee calculation endpoint with 19 unit tests, and verify the complete send flow end-to-end resolving port mapping errors and transfer proxy field mismatches.

Acceptance Criteria Resolution -- Security Logic Bug and Accessibility Fixes
A systematic audit against all 44 acceptance criteria revealed four gaps. The critical finding was a logic bug in the requires2FA helper: the function checked totpEnabled as its first gate, meaning when a participant had not configured TOTP the function returned false regardless of transfer amount, rendering the 2FA-required warning state completely unreachable. The fix removed totpEnabled from the helper entirely as the calling component already branches on that field separately. Three accessibility fixes added role="form" and aria-labelledby to the PIN entry section, role="alert" to the lockout container, and aria-live="assertive" to the remaining attempts counter. All four fixes totalled approximately 11 lines across two files.

Fiat Toggle Discoverability Redesign and Duplicate Elimination
Browser testing revealed the fiat currency toggle was effectively invisible. Across three iterations, the toggle evolved from a relocated icon to a Radix UI Switch with a "Favourite Currency" label and subtitle, positioned between the step indicator and form content. Live testing with fiat enabled revealed two currency selectors visible simultaneously; the embedded selector was replaced with a static read-only label establishing a single point of currency control.

Enterprise Brand Input System Migration
The send page used plain base input components while every other form used branded components with glass-morphism, kelly green focus rings, rounded-xl corners, and CSS-animated floating labels. The recipient, reason, and all three amount input fields were migrated to branded classes with floating labels using the peer-placeholder-shown and peer-focus CSS animation pattern. All three amount fields were converted from type="number" to type="text" with inputMode="decimal" to eliminate browser spinner arrows. All references to the plain input component were removed from the send page.

Global Design Token Architecture and iOS Font Size Resolution
A centralised design token layer was created in the global stylesheet. Mobile testing at 375 pixels revealed input values rendering at 16 pixels despite text-sm classes; the root cause was an iOS Safari zoom prevention hack forcing font-size: 16px !important on all inputs below 768 pixels. The fix replaced this with maximumScale: 1 in the Next.js viewport configuration. A @layer components block was created with reusable classes including gx-input, gx-input-label, error variants, and a layout system of gx-page, gx-page-grid, gx-card-header, gx-card-bar, gx-card-body, gx-form-stack, and gx-field-stack. Five branded field components were migrated from approximately 15 lines of className strings each to single cn('peer gx-input', ...) calls. Two API route bugs were corrected: a singular-to-plural path mismatch and a double /api/v1 prefix. Playwright verification across 320, 375, 768, and 1,280 pixel viewports confirmed a clean type hierarchy.

Comprehensive Ten-Agent Deep Audit and Systematic Remediation
A ten-agent audit examined architecture, security, accessibility, performance, design system compliance, state machine correctness, fiat conversion precision, TypeScript safety, end-to-end flow gaps, and API contract verification, returning 100-plus raw findings organised into six priority phases across three implementation tracks. Phase 1 addressed six critical security issues: a double-submit guard using both mutation pending check and useRef flag, a balance check bypass where balanceQirat === 0 was incorrectly passing, suspended recipient blocking, a NaN guard on the 2FA threshold comparison, fee-inclusive balance checking, and input validation on three security proxy routes. Phase 2 fixed five performance issues. Phase 3 replaced 33 design system token violations across seven files. Phase 4 added eight WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility improvements. Phase 5 added nine missing zero-decimal currencies and 55-plus country-to-currency mappings. Phase 6 hardened three areas including requestAnimationFrame replacement and console error sanitisation. The remediation produced 24 commits across 24 files with 158 insertions and 87 deletions.

Backend Fee Calculation Endpoint -- Clean Architecture Implementation
The wallet fee estimation had been returning "service unavailable" because the backend tokenomics service lacked the endpoint. A new endpoint was implemented with five new files and four modified files following Clean Architecture patterns. The use case implements pure BigInt arithmetic matching the chaincode fee formula: free tier below 3 GX, Tier 1 at 5 basis points for 3 to 100 GX, Tier 2 at 10 basis points above 100 GX, with a minimum 1-Qirat fee rule and overflow protection guard. A unit test suite of 19 cases covers all tier boundaries, the minimum Qirat rule, five input validation cases, and determinism, all passing in 10 milliseconds. Seven curl tests verified all tier calculations and authentication enforcement.

End-to-End Transfer Flow Verification and Integration Fixes
End-to-end verification via Playwright was conducted. The Kubernetes-deployed authentication service lacked participant resolution routes, resolved by running locally. A missing tenantId field in JWT tokens caused 401 responses. Five scrambled port mappings were corrected in the frontend environment configuration along with a duplicate variable override. The security verification step was modified to make PIN optional by removing the pin_not_set blocking state, respecting the database requirePinForTransfers field. Transfer submission failed due to complete field name mismatch between the frontend proxy and backend DTO; the proxy was rewritten with correct field mapping. The participant resolution endpoint was extended to include profileId. Three currency selector input bugs were fixed: an internal change loop, a missing internal change flag, and an over-firing effect. A blockchain-level transfer failure was diagnosed to a fundamental identifier mismatch where the transfer use case sent internal UUID instead of blockchain-native fabricUserId, carried forward to the next session.

Outcome: The send page was elevated from functional prototype to production-hardened status through four acceptance criteria fixes, an iterative UX overhaul with branded input consistency, a centralised design token architecture across ten files, systematic remediation of 100-plus audit findings, a Clean Architecture fee calculation endpoint with 19 unit tests, and end-to-end verification resolving five port mapping errors, a transfer proxy field mismatch, and a participant resolution data gap.

: March 7th 2026:
Wednesday - March 4, End-to-End Transfer Pipeline Completion: Blockchain Identity Resolution, Qirat-to-GX Unit Standardisation, Concurrent Transfer Architecture, and Full-Stack Audit with 42 New Tests

Objective: Resolve the critical blockchain identity mismatch preventing token transfers from reaching the ledger, fix a currency unit inconsistency across all CQRS projector event handlers corrupting wallet balances by a factor of one million, implement a concurrent transfer architecture with optimistic balance holds and cross-tab synchronisation, and execute a comprehensive security and code quality audit producing 42 new unit tests while redesigning the transfer receipt UX with live blockchain confirmation polling.

Critical Blockchain Identity Resolution in Transfer Pipeline
The day began with a transfer submission failure caused by a payload identity mismatch where the outbox command sent a wallet's internal UUID to the blockchain instead of the blockchain-native identity string. A new domain error (BlockchainIdentityNotFoundError, code BLOCKCHAIN_IDENTITY_NOT_FOUND, HTTP 422) was introduced. The transfer use case was corrected to resolve both sender and receiver blockchain identities through wallet associations. Fifteen previously uncommitted files from the prior session were committed across 10 backend commits, 6 frontend commits, and 2 parent repository submodule pointer updates. Full end-to-end verification confirmed the transfer committed to the blockchain at block 35. Two post-commit issues were identified: a missing status polling endpoint and a CQRS projector crash on transfer completion due to unmigrated fee collection tables.

Fee Collection Database Migration and Transfer Status Endpoint
The projector crash was caused by missing fee-related database tables. Four tables (FeeRate, FeeCollectionLog, FeeDistributionLog, FeeSubAccountBalance) were created directly in PostgreSQL with 22 indexes. A column width defect was corrected where feeCategory was defined as VARCHAR(10) but required VARCHAR(30) for composite names like p2p_cross_border. A complete Clean Architecture transfer status endpoint was built with an OutboxCommandStatus interface, findById repository method, Prisma implementation, GetTransferStatusUseCase, controller handler, route registration, and dependency injection wiring, verified via direct HTTP request returning committed status with transaction identifier and block number.

Qirat-to-GX Unit Mismatch Discovery and Projector-Wide Fix
Post-transfer, the wallet dashboard showed approximately one billion GX instead of one thousand. Wallets with only genesis allocations displayed correctly, isolating the problem to transfer processing. A systematic audit of every projector handler modifying the cached balance column confirmed the genesis distribution and velocity tax handlers correctly divided by the precision constant (1,000,000 Qirat per GX), but the transfer-with-fees, transfer-completed, internal-transfer, and loan-approved handlers wrote raw Qirat values directly. A shared QIRAT_PER_GX constant was created and all four broken handlers were fixed to perform division before writing, while two correct handlers were refactored to use the shared constant. Data correction was applied to all affected DevNet wallets with SQL verification confirming correct values across six wallets.

Transfer Flow E2E Audit and Status Vocabulary Fix
The frontend remained stuck on "Processing..." despite on-chain commitment. Investigation traced this to a status vocabulary mismatch: the backend returns COMMITTED while the frontend checks for CONFIRMED. A status mapping layer was introduced in the proxy route translating backend statuses to frontend equivalents. LocalStorage-based persistence was added for active transfers with five-minute expiry. The transaction card layout was redesigned promoting counterparty name to primary position and demoting payment reason. The transfer reason input was capped at 140 characters aligning with the SWIFT MT103 remittance field standard.

Cross-Border Fee Estimation Enhancement
Fee estimation consistently showed local rates even for cross-border transfers. The CalculateFeeUseCase was enhanced with a findCountryByProfileId repository method, a detectCrossBorder method resolving both parties' country codes in parallel, cross-border fee constants of 15 basis points up to 100 GX and 25 basis points above, and output enrichment with scope and rate. The design falls back silently to local rates when cross-border detection prerequisites are unavailable. Thirty-one unit tests were written covering five local tiers, four cross-border tiers, six fallback scenarios, five validation cases, and two determinism checks.

Concurrent Transfer Architecture Design and Implementation
A four-round brainstorming session produced specifications for concurrent transfer handling. Twenty-five architectural decisions covered optimistic frontend holds using localStorage, BroadcastChannel API for multi-tab synchronisation, a batch status endpoint collapsing N polling requests into one HTTP call, KYC tier-based daily limits, and human-readable reference numbers in the format GX-YYMMDD-HASH. Three standalone utility modules were created: a transfer reference generator, an error sanitisation engine matching chaincode error strings against regex patterns to produce user-friendly messages, and a structured transfer logger with correlation identifiers. The core useTransferHolds hook manages hold objects in React state backed by localStorage, synchronised across tabs via BroadcastChannel, polled via batch status every two seconds with terminal holds auto-dismissing after five seconds. The transfer flow was restructured so successful submission returns immediately to compose, allowing participants to initiate the next transfer while the previous confirms in the background.

Full-Stack Security and Code Quality Audit with UX Flow Redesign
A comprehensive audit surfaced three critical findings, seven high-severity, and eight medium-severity items. The most severe was an authentication bypass where an undefined requester identifier skipped the authorisation check, fixed by splitting into separate null guard (401) and ownership verification (403) checks. Request identifier collision risk from millisecond timestamps was replaced with cryptographic UUID generation. Floating-point arithmetic in GX-to-Qirat conversion was rewritten with string-based decimal arithmetic. The UX was redesigned: the step indicator and processing step were removed, and the receipt step shows immediately on submission with live status polling every two seconds auto-transitioning from "Transfer Submitted" to "Transfer Confirmed." Three test files containing 42 tests were written: 16 for Qirat conversion precision, 17 for error pattern matching, and 9 for reference format validation, bringing the total to 152 passing tests.

Database Corruption Correction and Auto-Poll Receipt UX
A 20 GX transfer from an earlier session had been recorded as 20,000,000 GX because the deployed projector ran the pre-fix image. Three transaction records, two wallet balances, and one fee collection log entry were corrected. Initial delta-based balance correction revealed the genesis handler had also stored Qirat as GX on these wallets, requiring full balance recalculation via aggregate SQL queries. A discrepancy check confirmed zero remaining inconsistencies. The receipt step was improved with automatic polling starting immediately on processing state detection.

Outcome: The complete token transfer pipeline was brought from non-functional to full end-to-end operability, encompassing blockchain identity resolution, currency unit standardisation across all six CQRS projector handlers, a concurrent transfer architecture with optimistic balance holds and cross-tab synchronisation, cross-border fee detection with 31 tests, a security audit with 42 new tests bringing the total to 152, enterprise-grade UX redesign with live blockchain confirmation polling, and database corruption correction with verified balance integrity across all DevNet wallets.

: March 7th 2026:
Thursday - March 5, Enterprise UI Quality Audit and 50-Plus Remediation Across Send Module, Government Treasury Specification Authoring with 26-Round Architectural Q&A, and Multi-Dimensional Spec Verification Producing Audited v2.0

Objective: Execute a systematic Playwright-driven visual audit of the send module across all viewport sizes remediating over 50 UI findings including two critical bugs, write a comprehensive 1,300-line government treasury module specification informed by 26 rounds of architectural decision-making, and verify the specification through an 11-agent audit against the actual codebase producing a corrected 1,535-line v2.0 with 26 new architectural decisions appended to the project decision register.

TypeScript Continuation Fixes and Compilation Verification
The session resolved two TypeScript errors from the prior session: a property access referencing profileId instead of the correct field name id, and two test mock files missing a fullName field. Both frontend and backend repositories were verified clean via npx tsc --noEmit. Nine commits across both repositories recorded the fullName feature addition: four backend commits for interface addition and test mock updates, plus five frontend commits for type update, self-send detection, profile identifier propagation, and fullName piping.

Playwright-Driven Visual Audit of Send Module
A systematic visual audit was conducted at 1440px desktop viewport testing seven scenarios. Self-send detection was verified working. Testing a non-active recipient exposed raw internal status strings displayed directly to users instead of human-readable labels. A valid cross-border transfer revealed a critical fee mismatch: the compose step correctly calculated 15 basis points but the review step independently called its own fee estimation hook passing a blockchain identifier instead of a profile identifier, falling back to the local 5 basis point rate. The security step showed oversized icons and exposed internal details. The receipt step used punitive language and exposed blockchain implementation details. Screenshots were captured at 375px, 768px, 1024px, and 1440px viewports. The analysis produced over 50 findings: 2 critical, 8 high, and 15-plus medium severity.

Multi-Agent Parallel Remediation of 50-Plus UI Findings
Four parallel agents with non-overlapping file assignments were deployed. The first agent fixed the critical fee mismatch by removing the independent fee estimation hook from the review step, converting it to consume parent-passed props as read-only values establishing a single source of truth. The second agent rewrote the receipt and security steps, changing "Total Deducted" to "Total Sent," replacing blockchain-specific waiting messages, and compacting the PIN-locked UI. The third agent addressed the compose step with right-aligned buttons, hidden character counter when empty, a STATUS_LABELS map translating database enums to user-friendly strings, and "Insufficient balance" display in red. The fourth agent handled flow orchestration, renaming "Favourite Currency" to "Local Currency," piping fullName to all child steps, and renaming "In-flight transfers" to "Pending Transfers." TypeScript compilation was verified clean. Ten per-file commits were created across 18 files modified and 19 total commits.

Government Treasury Module Specification
Three previously completed module specs were moved to a completed subdirectory. A comprehensive codebase audit examined all layers: the smart contract with 27 public functions, the backend with 80-plus endpoints, the administrative frontend with 9 routes, the user-facing wallet with 9 government routes, and the institutional admin centre with 14 routes. Twenty-six rounds of architectural Q&A resolved every open design question covering admin-driven onboarding, full context-switch model, inter-government lending expanding scope by 8-12 hours, dedicated transition wizard for elections, the principle that the protocol owner has zero involvement after activation, scoped JWT tokens, fee structure with intra-treasury free and external tiered rates, budget cycle workflow, and dual reconciliation strategy. The specification spanned 1,314 lines across 20 sections. Module size was upgraded from M (8-12 hours) to L-plus (55-78 hours across 15-22 sessions).

Multi-Dimensional Specification Audit and v2.0 Correction
Direct codebase inspection revealed significant factual discrepancies: every treasury lifecycle function name was wrong, three listed events did not exist while four actual events were missing, the administrative frontend was claimed to have 9 routes when only 2 existed, the backend had 102 endpoints across 18 route groups rather than the claimed 80-plus across 11, and the database schema contained 24 models not 15-plus. Six parallel correction agents deployed: one appending decisions D70-D95, one performing independent verification, one correcting sections 1-3, one adding security hardening sections, one expanding lending and transition sections, and one adding reconciliation models and API security. The spec grew from 1,314 to 1,535 lines with all factual corrections. The decisions register grew from 846 to 1,083 lines.

Institution Onboarding Module Audit and Architectural Planning
Preliminary work began on the institution onboarding module. Six parallel agents audited existing infrastructure: the smart contract with 12 public functions, the backend with 50-plus endpoints across 8 route groups, the institutional admin centre with 16-plus routes, the administrative frontend's five-tab organisation detail page, and the wallet's three-step creation wizard. Ten gaps were identified. Eight rounds of Q&A produced 31 new architectural decisions (D96-D126) covering entity model consolidation, five-step wizard expansion, spec-only deprecation strategy, sub-account inclusion with ceiling enforcement, flexible threshold multi-signature governance, 30-day session TTL, expanded API credential scopes, compliance module with beneficial ownership tracking, and the decision to split into core and advanced specifications given scope expansion from 14-18 hours to 60-100-plus hours.

Outcome: The send module was elevated to enterprise UI quality through systematic visual audit remediating over 50 findings including two critical bugs, a comprehensive 1,535-line government treasury specification was authored and verified against the actual codebase through multi-dimensional audit producing an authoritative v2.0 reference, and 57 new architectural decisions (D70-D126) were documented across government treasury and institution onboarding modules.

WEEK ENDING: A critical blockchain identity mismatch was discovered where the transfer pipeline sent an internal UUID to the ledger instead of the blockchain-native identity string, preventing all token transfers from reaching the chain. A systemic Qirat-to-GX unit inconsistency was found across four of six CQRS projector event handlers, which wrote raw Qirat values without dividing by the precision constant, inflating wallet balances by a factor of one million and corrupting DevNet data. Approximately 727 hardcoded colour violations were identified in the shared component layer that broke dark mode rendering across the wallet application. A logic bug in the requires2FA helper incorrectly gated on totpEnabled, rendering the two-factor authentication warning state completely unreachable for participants who had not configured TOTP. The fee estimation displayed incorrect rates on cross-border transfers because the review step independently called its own fee estimation hook with a blockchain identifier instead of a profile identifier, silently falling back to the local rate. A status vocabulary mismatch between backend (COMMITTED) and frontend (CONFIRMED) caused the transfer receipt to remain stuck on a processing indicator despite successful on-chain commitment.
column3: The blockchain identity mismatch was resolved by correcting the transfer use case to resolve both sender and receiver blockchain identities through wallet associations, introducing a dedicated BlockchainIdentityNotFoundError domain error with HTTP 422 status. The Qirat-to-GX corruption was fixed by creating a shared QIRAT_PER_GX constant and applying division in all four broken projector handlers, followed by manual SQL correction of all affected DevNet wallets verified through aggregate queries. The 727 dark mode violations were remediated through two waves of parallel agents targeting UI primitives, mega-components, and shared layers, reducing violations to 9 intentional exceptions with zero TypeScript errors. The 2FA logic bug was fixed by removing the totpEnabled gate from the helper function, as the calling component already branched on that field separately. The cross-border fee mismatch was resolved by removing the independent fee estimation hook from the review step and converting it to consume parent-passed props as read-only values, establishing a single source of truth. A status mapping layer was introduced in the proxy route to translate backend status vocabulary to frontend equivalents, and the receipt step was redesigned with automatic polling that transitioned live from submitted to confirmed state.

: March 7th 2026:
Sunday - March 8, Institution Onboarding Specification Suite, Dual-Write Balance Corruption Root Cause Resolution, SSO Architecture Full-Stack Implementation, and Multi-Agent Production Audit Remediation

Objective: Complete the full specification lifecycle for the institution onboarding module through a six-agent parallel codebase audit producing two enterprise-grade specification documents totalling 2,323 lines with 36 architectural decisions resolved, diagnose and resolve a persistent wallet balance corruption bug through forensic PostgreSQL statement-level tracing, implement the complete SSO architecture transformation across four codebases spanning OAuth2 PKCE authorisation through to cross-application token synchronisation, and execute a multi-agent production audit remediation of the transfer module including a critical security fix and 84 new tests across a 4,974-line specification verification pass.

Institution Onboarding Codebase Audit and Gap Analysis
Before specification authoring, two housekeeping items were resolved including an architectural decisions filename rename via git mv and stale cross-reference corrections, followed by pushing a backlog of 15 unpushed commits. Six research agents were deployed in parallel to audit separate system layers: chaincode analysis revealed 12 public functions, 4 data structures, and 11 event types with a complete lifecycle from Propose through Endorse to Activate; the backend audit discovered institution-related code scattered across four separate microservices with 50+ endpoints, 36 use cases, and 18+ database models; frontend audits across three applications found strong foundations at approximately 75-80% completion with 10 specific gaps identified and prioritised by severity including missing institution context cards in the hub and an insecure URL hash fragment authentication handoff.

Thirty-Six Architectural Decisions Across Ten Structured Rounds
The audit findings informed a rigorous Q&A process spanning ten structured rounds that produced 36 new architectural decisions. The foundational decision established that two parallel data models would be merged into a single canonical representation. Key decisions included expanding the onboarding wizard from 3 steps to 5, adopting threshold-based multi-signature governance rather than three-eyes approval, including sub-accounts within scope, implementing a full compliance module with beneficial ownership and sanctions screening, building a complete reporting suite with profit-and-loss and budget-versus-actual exports, and designing a webhook delivery system with HMAC-SHA256 signatures and exponential backoff retry. When accumulated scope exceeded the original 14-18 hour estimate, the module was split into a core phase covering onboarding, governance, and transfer flows, and an advanced phase covering sub-accounts, compliance, reporting, and webhooks.

Specification Authoring for Institution Onboarding Core and Advanced Phases
The core institution onboarding specification was written at 1,432 lines covering 19 sections plus 3 appendices, following the established enterprise format with audit summaries, architecture decision traceability, feature specifications with ASCII wireframes, an end-to-end verification matrix of 9 test scenarios, and 17 priority-zero acceptance criteria. Key sections detailed a 5-step onboarding wizard with auto-save, the full organisation lifecycle state machine, multi-signature governance, hub integration with context cards, and OAuth2 PKCE flow replacing the insecure hash fragment pattern. The advanced phase specification followed at 891 lines covering 13 sections plus 3 appendices, addressing sub-accounts with hierarchical parent-child structures limited to 3 levels, budget management with ceiling enforcement and carry-forward rules, a compliance module integrating sanctions screening with beneficial ownership tracking at a 25% threshold, a reporting suite spanning 9 report types with multi-format export, and a webhook delivery system supporting 11 event types with 4-attempt exponential backoff retry. Two additional audit agents confirmed approximately 65% of the advanced infrastructure was already built, revising the estimate downward from 40-55 hours to 30-42 hours.

Development Environment Reconstruction and Dual-Write Root Cause Discovery
The development environment was rebuilt against the Kubernetes cluster with port-forwards to PostgreSQL, wallet, admin, and trust services re-established. A complete transfer flow was executed successfully covering login, cross-border fee estimation at 15 basis points, transfer submission returning 202 Accepted, blockchain endorsement and commitment via the outbox worker, and projector event processing. Post-transfer balance inspection revealed persistent corruption where wallet balances displayed values inflated by a factor of one million. The breakthrough came from enabling PostgreSQL statement-level logging via log_statement = 'all' and monitoring all SQL statements in real-time during a triggered transfer. Two separate database connections were observed simultaneously updating the wallet table: the first executing relative decrement and increment operations with properly converted values from the projector handler, and the second executing absolute SET operations writing raw smallest-unit values directly to the standard-denomination column, overwriting the projector's correct updates. Two parallel search agents converged on the outbox-submitter worker's syncWalletBalanceFromBlockchain function, which queried the blockchain for the current balance in the smallest currency unit and wrote it directly to the database column storing values in the standard denomination without performing the division by one million. The fix added the unit conversion to both the modular and legacy versions of the function, and a misleading code comment was corrected.

SSO Architecture Full-Stack Implementation Across Four Codebases
The platform's authentication was transformed from three independent silos into a unified SSO system. The authentication service was elevated to a full OAuth2 Identity Provider with PKCE support through two Prisma schema migrations adding an admin authentication method enum and an OAuth authorisation code model. The dormant user session model was activated with a new repository port offering 9 lifecycle methods and a cryptographic token hasher using SHA-256. The login use case was rewritten to create session records with opaque refresh tokens, the refresh use case gained a dual-format handler accepting both legacy JWT and new opaque tokens with replay detection, and the logout use case was transformed from a no-op to real session revocation. Access token TTL was reduced from 24 hours to 15 minutes. A contexts API was built mapping 60+ granular database permission codes to specification-level equivalents. Four OAuth2 use cases were implemented: authorisation with PKCE challenge validation, code exchange with 6-step verification, OpenID Connect user information, and RFC 7009-compliant token revocation. A hardcoded three-client OAuth2 registry was created for the internal applications. The admin frontend was transformed into an OAuth2 PKCE client with a callback handler, cross-tab token synchronisation via BroadcastChannel, and dual-mode authentication. The wallet application was enhanced to serve as the identity provider's login interface with NextAuth.js configuration rewritten for 15-minute token refresh and an OAuth2 authorise proxy route. Admin promotion use cases were created with an SSO_LINKED password hash sentinel preventing legacy login bypass. Across all four codebases, approximately 37 new files were created and 30 existing files were modified with zero new TypeScript errors.

Multi-Agent Transfer Module Audit and Remediation
A comprehensive production-readiness audit of the transfer module evaluated all 89 acceptance criteria against the codebase, finding 65 met with 18 unmet items belonging to future phases. A new critical security vulnerability was identified where transfers could proceed without PIN verification when the PIN feature was not enabled. Six parallel implementation agents were orchestrated with non-overlapping file assignments deploying the balance corruption fix to Kubernetes via a Docker image build, implementing the PIN bypass security fix with a blocking state and redirect to security settings, correcting backend status mapping and error type narrowing, handling frontend UX polish including receipt call-to-action buttons and accessibility labels, and writing 84 new test cases across 6 files. Final verification confirmed zero TypeScript errors and 194 tests passing across both repositories.

Specification Verification Sprint Across Three Documents
A systematic verification pass was conducted across all three recently written specifications totalling 4,974 lines. A six-agent parallel audit of the two institution onboarding specifications checked structure, decision traceability, terminology compliance, codebase accuracy, and cross-document consistency. Corrections included updating event type counts from 9 to 11 to match the codebase, correcting organisation type enumerations, removing an incorrect port reference, adding missing lifecycle states, enforcing the currency prefix format convention, and aligning estimates between documents. The transfer module specification audit revealed a critical decision identifier collision where 25 identifiers overlapped with those already assigned to a different module in the master architectural decisions registry. All 25 were renamed to a module-local format. The audit also corrected an acceptance criteria count from 44 to 56 after manual recount and added an explanatory note for non-alphabetical section ordering.

Outcome: Two complete institution onboarding specifications totalling 2,323 lines were authored with 36 new architectural decisions, a persistent wallet balance corruption bug was resolved through forensic PostgreSQL tracing that identified a dual-write anti-pattern in the outbox-submitter worker, the full SSO architecture was implemented across four codebases with OAuth2 PKCE, session management, and admin promotion spanning approximately 67 files with zero new TypeScript errors, 84 new tests were written alongside a critical PIN bypass security fix for the transfer module, and a verification sprint across 4,974 lines of specification corrected 15+ issues including a critical decision identifier collision.

: March 7th 2026:
Monday - March 9, SSO Production Hardening with 21 Tiered Fixes, End-to-End OAuth2 Verification Sprint Achieving 6/6 Test Pass Rate, and Hub Module Backend Architecture Implementation

Objective: Harden the SSO architecture for production readiness by executing an 11-agent parallel verification sprint that identified 24 findings across five severity categories, implement all 21 actionable fixes across three tiers spanning security, frontend, and clean architecture compliance, validate the complete OAuth2 PKCE authentication flow through live end-to-end testing with Playwright browser automation discovering and resolving five integration bugs, and begin the post-login hub module by conducting a three-agent architecture audit and completing the backend phase with parallel query execution and backward-compatible API design.

Eleven-Agent Parallel Verification Sprint and Finding Classification
With all seven phases of the SSO implementation code-complete across four codebases, an 11-agent parallel verification sprint was launched auditing security via OWASP-style assessment, clean architecture compliance via port-and-adapter adherence, spec gap analysis validating all 56 acceptance criteria, QA test coverage, frontend UX, performance bottlenecks, end-to-end workflow tracing, dependency injection wiring, database schema correctness, test execution integrity, and documentation completeness. The sprint returned a verdict of functionally solid but requiring hardening: 67 out of 67 module unit tests passing, zero new TypeScript errors, 43 of 56 acceptance criteria fully implemented at 77%, end-to-end happy path validated across both frontend applications, and dependency injection wiring confirmed 100% correct with no circular dependencies. The agents collectively identified 24 findings: 9 high severity including plaintext credentials stored alongside hashes, three clean architecture violations where use cases imported database packages directly, JWT containing a roles field excluded by specification, missing refresh token replay detection, in-memory rate limiting that resets on pod restart, and a 401 interceptor bypassing the authentication store; 8 medium severity including non-timing-safe PKCE comparison, localStorage token storage surface, and sequential database queries; and 7 low severity items.

Three-Tier Production Hardening Implementation
All 21 fixes were implemented and verified through 45 individual commits across three repositories. Tier 1 addressed security with 7 fixes: the roles array was removed from the JWT payload per the minimal JWT specification, timing-safe comparison via crypto.timingSafeEqual was applied to PKCE code verification, domain error messages were sanitised to prevent PII leakage, granular rate limiters were deployed with login at 5 requests per minute, refresh at 20 per minute, and OAuth token exchange at 10 per minute, and SSO-linked admin accounts were blocked from the legacy login endpoint. Tier 2 addressed frontend fixes with 7 changes: the 401 interceptor was upgraded to a dual-mode refresh pattern using a registerRefreshDelegate function where SSO tokens refresh via the OAuth token endpoint while legacy tokens use the original path, non-admin participants were blocked after the SSO callback, BroadcastChannel cross-tab synchronisation was extended to broadcast the refresh token alongside the access token, stale OAuth2 request data was cleaned from sessionStorage, error feedback was added for SSO login initiation failure, callback error response parsing was improved with proper AxiosError handling, and cookie SameSite was set to Lax for OAuth cross-site redirect compatibility. Tier 3 addressed clean architecture compliance with 7 fixes: three use cases were refactored to use repository ports instead of direct PrismaClient access, the code exchange use case was updated to use the ITokenHasher port instead of importing Node.js crypto directly, the user information endpoint was enhanced with OIDC standard fields including given_name, family_name, and email_verified, and silent catch blocks were replaced with logger warnings.

Credit Scoring Integration Test Suite
A colleague completed the credit scoring module's integration test suite with 697 lines covering 22 test cases across 8 test groups: credit score calculation, recalculation, score retrieval with error handling, breakdown verification across 6 components, history tracking with trend analysis, tier-based lending eligibility, consent management covering the full grant-duplicate-list-revoke-re-grant cycle, dispute lifecycle through filing resolution and re-filing, and two full end-to-end lifecycle tests. Seven integration issues were encountered and resolved during the first test run including Prisma client regeneration, foreign key setup helpers, manual truncation of 7 credit-related tables, enum value correction from SCORE_ONLY to SCORE_AND_TIER, and a field name reference fix. The credit scoring module reached complete status with 22 of 22 integration tests passing.

End-to-End OAuth2 Verification with Live Services
A full end-to-end verification sprint was conducted using Playwright browser automation against live services with the authentication service and admin service running locally, both frontend applications on local development servers, and three backend services port-forwarded from Kubernetes. Four bugs were surfaced that no unit test or static analysis could detect: a 404 on the OAuth authorise route caused by a stale running process predating the route additions, a missing singular role field in the JWT required by shared HTTP middleware where the production hardening had removed the plural roles array but failed to add the singular role string, a 500 error during server-side token exchange caused by a missing AUTH_URL environment variable falling back to an unresolvable Kubernetes internal DNS name, and a SESSION_REVOKED error from Kubernetes pods running pre-SSO code. After resolution, the SSO dashboard rendered only skeleton loading states because the OAuth callback stored tokens but never fetched the admin profile since client-side navigation from callback to dashboard did not remount the layout. The fix was a single await refreshProfile() call inserted into the callback chain after token storage. The full test matrix of six scenarios then passed: legacy admin login, SSO login with complete OAuth2 PKCE ceremony, non-admin blocking verified through code review, token refresh in SSO mode tested by surgically modifying the stored access token's expiration via Playwright browser evaluation, two error scenarios verifying missing-parameters and fabricated-parameters handling, and standalone wallet login confirming blockchain-backed balance data with no SSO artifacts.

Hub Module Architecture Planning and Backend Implementation
Work commenced on the post-login hub module with a specification defining 1,349 lines and 64 acceptance criteria. A three-agent parallel audit validated a five-phase implementation plan: the spec gap agent confirmed 42 of 64 acceptance criteria explicitly covered at 66% which after incorporation rose to 62 of 64 at 97%, the codebase integration agent audited 19 critical integration points discovering a critical API proxy bug calling an incorrect backend path and a dependency injection gap with a missing repository parameter, and the security agent's findings were incorporated. The backend phase was completed with five files edited across the authentication service. New response DTOs were created for the structured hub contexts format including wallet context with action-required notifications, government context with ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes, institution context with organisation type and role, and administration context with pending review counts, alongside a unified hub response preserving the legacy flat contexts array for backward compatibility. The contexts use case was substantially rewritten with Promise.all for parallel execution of three concurrent queries, private helper methods extracted for admin detection and wallet notification derivation, and entity route mapping implemented directing government contexts to /government/{entityId}, business contexts to /entities/institutions/{entityId}, and NPO contexts to /entities/npo/{entityId}.

Outcome: The SSO architecture was hardened with 21 production fixes across three severity tiers validated by an 11-agent verification sprint, then verified end-to-end through Playwright-driven live service testing that uncovered and resolved five integration bugs invisible to unit testing, achieving a 6 of 6 pass rate across the complete test matrix including OAuth2 PKCE flow, token refresh, error handling, and standalone login scenarios. The hub module's backend phase was completed with parallel query execution and backward-compatible structured context responses. The credit scoring module reached full completion with 22 integration tests passing.

: March 7th 2026:
Tuesday - March 10, Post-Login Hub Module Full-Stack Implementation, E2E Verification Sprint, Production Readiness Remediation, Codebase Cleanup, and SSO Admin Promotion UI Rewrite

Objective: Complete the full-stack implementation of the post-login context-selection Hub module across backend and frontend layers, verify the feature end-to-end through browser automation testing, execute a comprehensive production readiness remediation addressing 16 audit findings across security, accessibility, and code quality categories, remove a superseded feature to eliminate dead code and TypeScript errors, and rewrite the administrative user creation interface from a legacy password-based form to a participant-promotion model aligned with the SSO architecture.

Hub Frontend Component Implementation and Routing Integration
The Hub module's frontend layer was built upon the backend use case returning both a legacy flat context array and a structured response with wallet, government, institutions, and administration groupings. A critical API proxy bug was fixed where the frontend identity client called an incorrect route path. A TanStack Query hook was implemented with aggressive cache invalidation at staleTime of zero, five-minute garbage collection time, and two retries with one-second delay. Internationalisation-ready string constants were created covering greeting templates, section labels, card descriptions, notification labels, and error messages. Three distinct card components were built: an internal navigation card for wallet, government, business, and organisation contexts using client-side routing with a double-click guard; a command centre card navigating to the administration portal triggering SSO auto-flow with error handling and toast fallback; and an institutional administration card opening an external portal in a new tab without exposing tokens. Each card was assigned a distinct icon and colour mapping with full accessibility support including list item roles, tab index, keyboard navigation via Enter and Space, focus-visible ring indicators, hover shadow elevation, and active press scaling.

Hub Page Architecture with Five-Guard Safety Net
The Hub page was constructed with a five-guard system: an onboarding safety net deferring to the layout's own check, a loading state rendering a skeleton matching the Hub layout, an error state with retry when no cached data exists, a context-count guard skipping users with one or fewer contexts directly to the dashboard using a ref to prevent re-renders, and a zero-contexts edge case fallback. The page renders two sections: a Wallets and Accounts section always appearing with personal wallet and institutional cards, and an Administration section conditionally rendering when government or admin contexts exist. The layout uses a responsive grid with single-column mobile and two-column tablet and desktop breakpoints with fade-in entrance animation.

Middleware, Routing, Navigation, and Unit Tests
The application middleware was updated to include the Hub route, its sub-paths, the government route group, and a context-selector redirect catching the legacy route to prevent back-button loops. The authenticated user redirect target was changed from the onboarding flow to the Hub. A new Hub entry was added to the sidebar navigation with a layout grid icon, and the legacy context-selector page was replaced with a redirect. Twelve unit tests were written for the backend use case covering wallet-only participants, government treasury administrators, admin roles with notification counts, combined contexts, business roles, backward-compatible flat array matching, null email handling, profile-not-found graceful fallback, onboarding status, inactive admin skipping, deleted admin skipping, and parallel query execution verification. All twelve tests passed with zero TypeScript errors across both compilations. Twenty-three file-by-file commits were made across three repositories.

E2E Verification Sprint and Schema Drift Discovery
An end-to-end verification sprint using browser automation surfaced three cascading issues: a port conflict where a shared environment variable overrode the authentication service's default port, a shell escaping problem where special characters triggered bash history expansion requiring a Python URL library workaround, and a Prisma schema drift where a column added by another developer's compliance work did not exist in the actual database causing a 500 error on the contexts endpoint. The schema drift was resolved by adding error-tolerant handling to the institutional roles query. Seven of ten E2E test cases passed including login as a multi-context user rendering the Hub with personalised greeting, navigating from a wallet card to the dashboard with balance display, re-navigating to the Hub confirming fresh data refetch, and a full SSO cross-application flow through to the administration portal dashboard. Three tests were deferred due to the absence of a wallet-only test account and schema drift preventing government treasury card testing.

Production Readiness Remediation Across Three Codebases
A five-track production readiness audit identified twenty findings across security, code quality, accessibility, and specification compliance categorised as two critical, six high, eight medium, and four low severity. Sixteen findings were remediated across nineteen files in three submodules. The first critical fix removed a plaintext OAuth authorisation code persisted as a database column, eliminating the field from the schema, port interface, repository implementation, and both use cases with two unit tests added confirming codes are neither persisted nor read. The second critical fix addressed dynamic CSS class construction causing the build tool's JIT engine to miss hover classes in production by introducing a pre-composed hover colour property. Six high-severity fixes addressed a CORS wildcard fallback replaced with an explicit origin allowlist, development credentials gated behind an environment check, an open redirect vulnerability resolved with a URL validation utility applied across three authentication files, an unsafe error type assertion replaced with instanceof narrowing, a hardcoded string extracted to a constant, and a specification deviation documented. Eight medium-severity fixes addressed a double-click race condition, dead unreachable guard code, missing ARIA role descriptions and labels, missing loading semantics, a weak unknown type strengthened, a non-exhaustive switch statement made exhaustive, and verbose aggregation simplified. Verification confirmed ninety-five of ninety-five backend unit tests passing and zero TypeScript errors.

Duplicate Feature Removal and Codebase Cleanup
An audit revealed two overlapping features for managing business, government, and non-profit entities: an incomplete organisations feature built by another developer that called a non-existent backend endpoint, was hidden from the sidebar, had a broken creation flow, and carried nineteen pre-existing TypeScript errors, alongside a production-ready entity-accounts feature fully wired to the backend. Dependency analysis confirmed clean external boundaries with no imports from outside the organisations directory. Thirteen files totalling 3,544 lines were removed comprising type definitions, query and mutation hooks, query keys, route pages, and five tab components. TypeScript compilation confirmed zero organisation-related errors after removal.

SSO Admin Promotion UI Design and Full-Stack Implementation
A request to create a new administrator exposed a fundamental architectural mismatch where the portal still displayed a legacy password-based creation form while the SSO module mandates a participant-promotion model. A backend audit confirmed promotion and promotable search endpoints were implemented but the frontend never used them. Seven backend files were modified to extend the promotable search output with blockchain identity, country code, and creation date, add blockchain identity to the repository search clause, add optional custom permissions to the promotion DTO and use case, and thread permissions through the controller. Four frontend files were changed including a full rewrite of the creation page from a password-based form to a search-and-promote flow with debounced search input, participant selection cards with status badges, a role dropdown filtered by the assigner's hierarchy level, and a collapsible permissions section with category-grouped checkboxes displaying risk level badges and role-match detection.

Outcome: The post-login Hub module was completed end-to-end with twelve unit tests and seven of ten E2E test cases verified including a full cross-application SSO flow. Production readiness remediation resolved all critical and high-severity findings totalling sixteen fixes across nineteen files. A total of 3,544 lines of dead code were removed by eliminating a superseded feature. The administration creation interface was fully rewritten from a legacy password-based form to a participant-promotion model aligned with the SSO architecture. Across five sessions, fifty-eight files were changed with forty-eight commits spanning three repositories, with ninety-five backend unit tests passing and zero TypeScript errors.

: March 7th 2026:
Thursday - March 12, ESLint-Driven Component Architecture Refactoring, E2E Admin Promotion Verification, RBAC API Response Bug Discovery and Fix, Security Hardening of Admin Creation Pipeline, OAuth2 PKCE SSO Flow Debugging, Cross-App Logout Architecture, and Hub Sidebar Integration

Objective: Resolve ESLint and React Compiler strictness violations through progressive architectural refactoring of a monolithic admin creation page into a component hierarchy, execute end-to-end Playwright testing of the admin promotion flow uncovering a systemic API response wrapping bug, harden the admin creation pipeline by closing four security gaps, debug and resolve a five-layer OAuth2 PKCE SSO flow, design and implement a cross-application logout architecture using redirect chains, and integrate the Hub link into the administration portal sidebar.

ESLint Remediation as Progressive Architectural Refactoring
What initially appeared as routine linter error resolution evolved into four rounds of progressively deeper architectural refinement driven by React Compiler strictness rules. The admin creation page began as a 427-line monolith exceeding the 300-line maximum with a cyclomatic complexity of 14 against a maximum of 10, alongside six errors and twenty-eight warnings. The first round extracted visual components reducing the file to 397 lines but exposing memoisation dependency errors where optional chaining on sub-properties produced unstable references. The second round created section components and fixed memoisation dependencies but revealed setState-in-effect errors where two useEffect blocks called setState synchronously. The third round eliminated these useEffect anti-patterns by replacing the auto-select effect with a derived value pattern using a pure function and replacing the clear-on-change effect by handling it directly in the search change callback. An attempted useRef approach was rejected by the React Compiler's refs rule prohibiting ref access during render. The fourth round extracted a custom permissions editor hook using render-time state adjustment tracking the last synced role in state rather than using an effect. The final architecture comprises six files totalling 580 lines: a 133-line orchestrator page, a 63-line participant card, a 52-line permission checkbox group, a 114-line search section, a 134-line role permissions section, and an 84-line permissions editor hook. All six original errors and twenty-two of twenty-eight warnings were resolved.

Enterprise-Discipline Commits and Gap Analysis
Nineteen commits were made across three repositories with each file receiving its own commit and descriptive message. Seven backend commits covered the extended promotable search output, repository search additions, custom permissions in the promotion schema and use case, the repository port method addition, controller simplification, and module registration. Nine frontend commits covered type definitions, admin hooks, four new component files, the permissions editor hook, the rewritten page orchestrator, and a button label update. Three parent repository commits recorded submodule updates and session documentation. All nineteen commits passed lint-staged hooks on the first attempt. A comprehensive gap analysis using three parallel verification tracks confirmed one hundred percent implementation completeness across all eleven planned backend items and all eighteen planned frontend features with API contracts verified as aligned.

E2E Playwright Testing and Systemic RBAC Response Wrapping Bug
End-to-end testing promoted a test participant to the highest administrative role. Initial testing with a standard admin account correctly demonstrated role hierarchy enforcement preventing same-rank assignment. After logging in as the super owner account, the search, participant card rendering, and role selection functioned correctly with single-result auto-selection working via the derived value pattern. However, expanding the permissions customisation section revealed a critical bug: permission count displayed zero instead of eighty-plus permissions, the role match indicator showed a custom label instead of the correct role name, and no checkboxes rendered. Investigation traced the data flow through the entire chain from backend to TanStack Query cache, revealing that fetch functions returned the API response wrapper rather than the inner data. The root cause was systemic across all eight RBAC fetch functions. The fix applied the response unwrapping pattern to all eight functions: permissions listing, category-filtered permissions, single permission details, role summaries, role details with permissions array, the cross-role permission matrix, current admin effective permissions with a type mapping fix for legacy and current response shapes, and specific admin permission summaries. This fix repaired both the new promotion page and the existing roles management page which had contained the same latent bug.

Successful Admin Promotion E2E Verification
After deploying the response unwrapping fix, the Playwright flow was re-executed. The participant search returned correct data, the role defaulted to the highest administrative level, and permissions customisation loaded seventy-one permissions across six categories: User with fourteen, Financial with nineteen, Audit with fourteen, System with twenty-eight, Configuration with fourteen, and Deployment with seven. The promotion was submitted successfully, redirecting to the admin list where the promoted user appeared with the correct role and active status, with the admin count increasing from three to four.

Security Hardening of the Admin Creation Pipeline
Four security gaps were identified and addressed. The first critical gap was a legacy appointment endpoint that accepted password-based admin creation without requiring a participant profile, with the admin repository auto-generating a user profile to bypass KYC and blockchain identity verification; the route was disabled with the use case code preserved but unreachable. The second critical gap was the absence of role hierarchy enforcement in the promotion use case; the fix extracted the promoter's role from the JWT context and added hierarchy validation matching the pattern used by the legacy appointment flow. The third high-severity gap was that custom permissions accepted arbitrary strings with no validation; the fix added a pre-creation step fetching all system permissions, building a valid code set, and rejecting unknown codes. A fourth finding regarding KYC field naming was investigated and confirmed as a false positive. Nine commits across two repositories captured these security fixes.

OAuth2 PKCE SSO Flow End-to-End Debugging
Making the Hub-to-administration-portal navigation trigger a seamless OAuth2 PKCE authentication flow required resolving five layers of issues. The command centre card was fixed to add an SSO trigger parameter, with the portal login form detecting this parameter and auto-initiating SSO using a lazy useState initialisation pattern. The OAuth authorise proxy route produced a 500 error diagnosed through server log inspection as five cascading problems: an HTTP method mismatch where the proxy sent POST but the backend expected GET with query parameters, an expired access token where the raw cookie reader did not trigger JWT refresh requiring a switch to the session handler, and a database schema drift where a NOT NULL constraint on a plaintext authorisation code column conflicted with the hash-only storage design requiring a manual column alteration and index removal. After resolution, the full seventeen-step SSO flow was verified end-to-end through browser automation: from Hub card click through PKCE parameter generation, redirect to the wallet login with OAuth parameters, session detection and authorise call with fresh token, auth code generation with hash-only storage, redirect to the portal callback with code and state, PKCE verification and token exchange, and final dashboard rendering. The total redirect chain completed in approximately two to three seconds.

Hub Layout Migration, Hydration Fix, Cross-App Logout, and Sidebar Integration
The Hub page was migrated from the main client route group to a standalone route group with a minimal authentication-protected layout, ensuring it renders as a clean context selector without dashboard navigation elements. A React hydration mismatch in the SSO callback was fixed by removing an unnecessary server-side guard from a component already wrapped in a Suspense boundary, where useMemo accessed sessionStorage with a typeof window check producing divergent HTML trees. A cross-application logout architecture was designed after evaluating three approaches: shared backend session revocation rejected for infrastructure overhead, postMessage via hidden iframe rejected for CORS complexity, and a redirect chain approach selected for simplicity. Two new endpoints were created: a server-side API route in the wallet application iterating over all NextAuth cookies and expiring them before redirecting, and a client-side page in the administration portal clearing localStorage tokens and broadcasting logout to other tabs. Both existing logout handlers were modified to redirect through the other application's remote-logout endpoint with return URL validation against a strict origin allowlist. The Hub link was added to the administration portal sidebar under the Dashboard section using the LayoutGrid icon. TypeScript compilation passed in both applications with zero owned errors.

Outcome: A monolithic 427-line admin creation page was refactored into a six-file component architecture satisfying all React Compiler strictness rules. A systemic RBAC API response wrapping bug was discovered during E2E testing and fixed across all eight fetch functions repairing both the new promotion page and an existing roles management page. The first participant was successfully promoted to super administrator through the new SSO-aligned flow. Four security gaps in the admin creation pipeline were closed including legacy endpoint disablement and role hierarchy enforcement. The full seventeen-step OAuth2 PKCE SSO flow from Hub to administration portal was debugged and verified end-to-end. A cross-application logout architecture using redirect chains was designed and implemented across both applications, and the Hub was integrated into the administration portal sidebar. Thirty commits were made across three repositories with all TypeScript and ESLint checks passing.
